parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject a malicious `dir` value, let the query builder concatenate it, then execute the crafted SQL.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers may read or modify data, or execute arbitrary SQL if the driver allows stacked statements.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can set `dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and the f-string builds unsafe SQL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/list_users?dir=asc;DROP TABLE users--` and observe the raw query contains attacker SQL.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `dir` is interpolated into SQL without validation, enabling SQL injection in `ORDER BY`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Supply an internal target URL, let the server fetch it, and return the response body to the attacker.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read internal services, cloud metadata, and other network-only resources from the server.
parsed_findings.proof: `req.query.url` flows directly into `axios.get(u)` and the only guard is a broken blacklist check on line 3.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` or another internal URL and observe the server fetch it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint is an unauthenticated SSRF proxy because it fetches any user-supplied URL with axios.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass the single replace, then use enough `../` segments to escape `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read sensitive files outside the intended download directory.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../../../etc/passwd` becomes `../../../etc/passwd`, and `path.join` resolves outside `/srv/data`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../../../etc/passwd` and read host files if permissions allow.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Naive `../` stripping still allows path traversal and arbitrary file read.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Set `state` to attacker-controlled base64 pickle; request `/resume`; payload executes during `pickle.loads`.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution or full server compromise under the app's privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can forge `state` with a base64-pickled payload and trigger deserialization on `/resume`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `/resume` with a `state` cookie containing base64 of a malicious pickle object.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use this IDOR to target a victim account, then exploit the mass-assignment bug to overwrite protected fields.
parsed_findings.impact: Any authenticated user can edit other accounts, enabling account takeover or privilege changes.
parsed_findings.proof: It loads `db.users.findById(req.params.id)` and saves it with no authorization check tied to `req.user`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate as user A, PATCH `/api/users/B` with any body, and observe user B is modified.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler updates any user by `:id` without checking that the caller owns that account or has admin rights.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pair this with the unrestricted `:id` update to modify another user's protected fields.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can escalate privileges or corrupt sensitive account attributes.
parsed_findings.proof: The request body is merged directly into `user` and then saved, so any writable property can be set by the client.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a PATCH body like `{ "isAdmin": true, "role": "admin" }` and the saved user will inherit those fields.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` blindly copies attacker-controlled fields onto the persisted user object.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-862","file":"users.js","line_start":1,"line_end":7,"vuln_claim":"The handler updates any user by `:id` without checking that the caller owns that account or has admin rights.","proof":"It loads `db.users.findById(req.params.id)` and saves it with no authorization check tied to `req.user`.","repro_steps":"Authenticate as user A, PATCH `/api/users/B` with any body, and observe user B is modified.","impact":"Any authenticated user can edit other accounts, enabling account takeover or privilege changes.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Use this IDOR to target a victim account, then exploit the mass-assignment bug to overwrite protected fields."},{"cwe":"CWE-915","file":"users.js","line_start":6,"line_end":7,"vuln_claim":"`Object.assign(user, req.body)` blindly copies attacker-controlled fields onto the persisted user object.","proof":"The request body is merged directly into `user` and then saved, so any writable property can be set by the client.","repro_steps":"Send a PATCH body like `{ \"isAdmin\": true, \"role\": \"admin\" }` and the saved user will inherit those fields.","impact":"An attacker can escalate privileges or corrupt sensitive account attributes.","confidence":0.97,"chain_steps":"Pair this with the unrestricted `:id` update to modify another user's protected fields."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit the command injection in `name` to execute arbitrary OS commands.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution as the web app user.
parsed_findings.proof: `name='x; id >/tmp/pwned; #'` makes the shell run `id` when the route builds `tar -czf /tmp/x; id >/tmp/pwned; #.tgz ...`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON with a malicious `name` value and any allowed `src`, then check for the injected command's side effect.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use traversal to target sensitive paths outside `/var/data/`, then archive and exfiltrate them.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file read and exfiltration through the backup archive.
parsed_findings.proof: `src='/var/data/../../etc'` passes `startswith('/var/data/')` but makes `tar` operate on `/etc` after path resolution.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `src` with `../` segments under `/var/data/` and observe the backup include files outside the intended directory.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `/var/data/` prefix check is lexical only and can be bypassed with traversal in `src`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Store the payload at line 5, then the `/comments` response exposes it through line 10.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can run JS in readers' browsers and steal sessions or perform actions as them.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Send a negative transfer amount, then observe the sender balance rise and the recipient balance fall.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can mint funds or drain another account without authorization.
parsed_findings.proof: With `amt=-1`, line 8 adds 1 to the sender and line 9 subtracts 1 from `to`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `{"to":"victim","amount":-1}` as any logged-in user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Negative `amount` values are accepted, letting an attacker credit their own balance and debit the recipient.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger verify failure, hit the production fallback, and use the decoded claims as authenticated identity.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass JWT authentication and impersonate any user or role encoded in the payload.
parsed_findings.proof: A token with any payload and an invalid signature reaches `jwt.decode(t)` in production and returns attacker-controlled claims.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `Authorization: Bearer <forged JWT>` that fails `jwt.verify`; in production the function still returns decoded claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after verification fails, so forged JWTs are accepted without a valid signature.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use timing differences to infer the hex digest, then resend the same body with the recovered `X-Sig`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can forge webhook requests and trigger `process_event()` without knowing `SECRET`.
parsed_findings.proof: Python string equality short-circuits on mismatches, so `got == want` can reveal how many prefix bytes are correct.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with candidate `X-Sig` prefixes and measure response timing to recover the expected digest.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Using `==` to compare the provided signature leaks timing and can let an attacker recover a valid HMAC.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject query operators through the login form to satisfy `findOne` without knowing valid credentials.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bypass authentication and gain unauthorized access to accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: If `email` or `password` is an object like `{"$ne":null}`, `findOne(q)` can match an arbitrary user instead of exact credentials.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with JSON body `{"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` and observe an account lookup succeed.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.body` is copied straight into a database query, enabling NoSQL operator injection and login bypass.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflowing the buffer can overwrite control data such as the saved return address.
parsed_findings.impact: This can crash the program or enable code execution on the stack.
parsed_findings.proof: `buf` is only 64 bytes and `gets` performs no length check before writing.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send more than 63 bytes on stdin to overflow `buf` and corrupt adjacent stack data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, causing a stack overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Obtain code access, extract KEY, then decrypt captured ciphertexts.
parsed_findings.impact: All encrypted data is exposed and the same key can be reused across installs.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 defines a fixed 16-byte key reused by enc() on line 5.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read the source, copy KEY, then decrypt any iv+ciphertext blob produced here.

parsed_findings.impact: This can crash the process and cause a denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` returns on non-NULL out, then line 5 copies into `out` when it is NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("a", NULL, 8)` and it reaches `memcpy(out, in, n)` with a NULL destination.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard is inverted, so valid non-NULL outputs are rejected and NULL out can reach memcpy.
